name: Zayd ibn al-Haritha
title: Zayd ibn al-Haritha (RA): The Beloved of the Beloved
meta_description: The story of Zayd ibn al-Haritha (RA), the boy who was kidnapped and sold into slavery, then chose the Prophet Muhammad over his own family, and became the only companion named in the Quran.
keywords: Zayd ibn al-Haritha, story of Zayd, the beloved of the Prophet, freed slave Islam, companion named in the Quran, sahaba stories
lede: A boy travelled with his mother to see her family. They stopped for the night, and in the dark a group of raiders took everything they had, including the boy. By morning he was on his way to a slave market, and his parents would spend years not knowing whether he was alive.

This is the story of Zayd ibn al-Haritha (may Allah be pleased with him): the child who was stolen and sold, the man who chose the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ over his own freedom and family, and the only companion ever named by name in the Quran.

sections.h2: A boy taken in the night
paras: Zayd was an Arab boy, not a captive of war. His father, Haritha, came from a respected tribe outside Makkah, and his mother, Suda, came from her own people. He was about ten years younger than the Prophet ﷺ, and those who knew him later described him as short, of very dark skin, with a slight build.

When he was still a child, he set out with his mother to visit her relatives. They camped for the night along the road. While they slept, a band of raiders fell on the tent, took their food and money, and carried Zayd away from his mother. He remembered the night precisely for the rest of his life.

The men sold him quickly into the slave market of Makkah, the way the Prophet Yusuf had once been sold for a few coins. The plan was simple: sell the child fast, before anyone could trace where he had come from, then disappear. From hand to hand he passed, while far away his parents began to search.

Haritha could not find his son. He went out by day looking for him, and he wrote poetry about his loss, the kind of verse that lets you feel a father's grief across the centuries. He would sit outside his door and weep.

He wrote that he wept for Zayd not knowing what had become of him: was he alive, so that one day he might hope to see him again, or had death already taken him? He said he only wanted closure. He asked, by God, whether his son had died on open ground or whether a mountain had been his end.

He did not want a guess. He wanted to know how, and where, so that he could begin to grieve. For years there was no answer. His son was alive, in a market in Makkah, and no one could trace the path back to him.

Khadijah (may Allah be pleased with her) was the wealthiest woman in Makkah, but she did not live surrounded by servants. One day she asked her nephew Hakim ibn Hizam to find her someone to help in the house. He went to the same slave market, saw the small, unremarkable boy, and bought him for four hundred dirhams. He brought Zayd to Khadijah, and she took him into her home.

When Khadijah married the Prophet ﷺ, Zayd came with that household. A bond formed almost at once. The Prophet ﷺ loved him and saw something in his character and his quiet manner. Khadijah noticed it, and one day she asked her husband whether he loved Zayd. He said yes. "Then he is yours," she said, and gave the boy to him.

The Prophet ﷺ did not treat Zayd as someone beneath him. By then Zayd was about fifteen, not a small child, yet he was raised as a son in that house, no different from the Prophet's own children. This was years before any revelation came. The man who showed this boy such honour was, as yet, only known to Makkah as the trustworthy one.

Years passed. Haritha never stopped looking, and one day his brother saw Zayd near the Kaaba and recognised him. Haritha hurried to Makkah, found the Prophet ﷺ sitting by the Kaaba, and made his appeal: your people are known for generosity and for caring for those in need; my son was taken unjustly; name any price and I will pay it to free him.

The Prophet ﷺ offered something better than a price. Let Zayd choose, he said. If he wants to go with you, take him, and pay nothing. So Zayd was called, and he confirmed that these were his father and his uncle. Then the Prophet ﷺ told him he was free to leave with them, with no ransom at all.

Zayd answered without pause. "I will not choose anyone over you," he said. "You are to me as father and mother." His own father was stunned. Would he really choose slavery over freedom, over his family, over his home? But Zayd was not speaking about status. He said he had seen something in this man that he could never turn away from.

sections.h2: A son, freely given a name
paras: The Prophet ﷺ understood what the father truly wanted. Haritha was not asking for his grown son to come live with him; he was worried about his son's standing in society, the simple fact that he was counted as a slave. So the Prophet ﷺ took Zayd to the steps of the Kaaba and announced before the people: bear witness that Zayd is free, that he is my son, that he inherits from me and I from him.

At that, the father was content. The announcement settled Zayd's place as a free man, and Zayd was content too, for he would not have left in any case. From that day he was known as Zayd, son of Muhammad ﷺ, a free man and, in the eyes of his society, a son of the Prophet ﷺ. He went on to marry into noble families, treated as a full member of the community.
Years later, the Quran would describe Zayd not as a slave but as the man whom God and His Prophet had favoured, the only companion named by name in the whole of the Book:

ayah.text: When you [Prophet] said to the man who had been favoured by God and by you, 'Keep your wife and be mindful of God,' you hid in your heart what God would later reveal: you were afraid of people, but it is more fitting that you fear God. When Zayd no longer wanted her, We gave her to you in marriage so that there might be no fault in believers marrying the wives of their adopted sons after they no longer wanted them. God's command must be carried out:

sections.h2: The only one beside him at Taif
paras: Zayd was among the first to accept Islam, recognised as the first freed man to believe. He stayed in the Prophet's house as a son, and so he was there for the hardest day of the Prophet's life. After Khadijah and Abu Talib had died and Makkah had turned openly hostile, the Prophet ﷺ went to the town of Taif to seek shelter and was refused at every door.

When he left, the people lined up two rows of children and others and made him walk between them, striking and spitting at him for a long stretch of road. He fell more than once. Zayd was the only one with him, trying to step in front of the blows, taking the beating himself to shield the Prophet ﷺ. When it was over, the Prophet ﷺ sat beneath a tree and turned to his Lord, asking, with the blood still fresh, who he was being left to. The angel of the mountains offered to crush the towns that had done this, and the Prophet ﷺ refused, hoping their children might one day worship God alone.
Aisha (may Allah be pleased with her) once asked the Prophet ﷺ what the worst day of his life had been. He did not name a battle. He named Taif. Zayd was the one who was there for it.

In Madinah, Zayd was paired in brotherhood with Hamza, the Prophet's uncle, and the two grew very close. Zayd was a gifted warrior and one of the Prophet's archers. He fought in the major battles and stood beside the Prophet ﷺ at Hudaybiyyah. On every military expedition the Prophet ﷺ sent him on, he placed him in command.

His son Usama was as beloved to the Prophet ﷺ as Zayd was, the beloved son of the beloved one, raised alongside the Prophet's own grandchildren. When the Prophet ﷺ wanted to break the old hierarchies of tribe and class, he married people across those lines, and Zayd's own marriages were part of that. He even married Umm Ayman, the woman who had cared for the Prophet ﷺ as a child, after the Prophet ﷺ said whoever wished to marry a woman of Paradise should marry her.

Zayd was given command of the army at the battle of Mutah, where some three thousand Muslims met a far larger force. He was killed there, along with Jafar ibn Abi Talib and Abdullah ibn Rawahah. The news did not come by messenger. The angel Jibril brought it first, and the Prophet ﷺ climbed the pulpit to announce the loss, weeping as he told his people that his son Zayd was gone.

lessons_intro: Zayd had every reason to choose comfort, status, and his own family. He kept choosing something quieter and harder, and that is why his story endures.

lessons.text: The Prophet ﷺ honoured Zayd as a son years before any revelation came. Good treatment of those in your care is not a reward for faith; it is the ground faith grows in.

lessons.bold: Loyalty is shown when leaving is easy.
lessons.text: Zayd could have walked away free, to his father and his home. He stayed because of what he had seen in one man's character, and chose him over his own freedom.

lessons.bold: Belonging is not about blood or status.
lessons.text: A kidnapped boy of no rank became a son, a commander, and a name in the Quran. People are valued for what is in them, not where they came from.

lessons.bold: Real closeness asks more of you, not less.
lessons.text: Being near the Prophet ﷺ meant Zayd was at Taif and at Mutah, not sheltered from hardship but drawn deeper into it. He stood in front of the blows.

faq.a: An Arab boy who was kidnapped as a child and sold into slavery in Makkah, then brought into the household of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. He was freed, raised as a son, and became one of the first to accept Islam and a trusted commander of the Muslim army.

faq.q: Why is Zayd called the beloved of the Prophet?
faq.a: The Prophet ﷺ loved him deeply and treated him as his own son, and Zayd's son Usama was equally dear to him. His nickname was the beloved one, and his son was called the beloved son of the beloved.

faq.q: Why did Zayd choose to stay with the Prophet instead of his own father?
faq.a: When his father came to free him, the Prophet ﷺ let Zayd choose. Zayd said he would not choose anyone over the Prophet ﷺ, because of the character he had seen in him. In response the Prophet ﷺ freed him publicly and declared him a son.

faq.q: What can we learn from the life of Zayd?
faq.a: That character is recognised before any words are spoken, that true loyalty shows itself when leaving would be easy, and that belonging is never about blood or rank.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: Zayd was given a clear path back to freedom and family, and he turned it down for something he could only describe as a quality he had seen in one man. We are rarely asked to make a choice that dramatic. But we are asked, almost every day, to choose between what looks better and what is better: between status and sincerity, between comfort and the people and principles we know are right.

His life is an invitation to notice character, in others and in ourselves, and to let it weigh more than rank or appearance. It is an invitation to be loyal when loyalty costs something, and to stand in front of the blows for the people we love rather than behind them. Carry one of those into today, and his story has done its work.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Zayd ibn al-Haritha (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). The Quran translation is from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Quran 33:37). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

social.caption:
He was kidnapped as a child and sold into slavery, far from a family that would search for him for years. His name was Zayd ibn al-Haritha (may Allah be pleased with him).

When his father finally found him, the Prophet ﷺ gave him a free choice: leave with no ransom, or stay. Zayd chose to stay, saying he would never choose anyone over the Prophet ﷺ. He was freed, named a son, and stood beside the Prophet ﷺ on the hardest day of his life. He is the only companion named by name in the Quran.

Which part of his story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

Read the full story: buruja.com/companions/zayd-ibn-al-haritha

email.subject: The boy who chose the Prophet over his own freedom
email.alt_subject: "I will not choose anyone over you"
email.preview: Kidnapped, sold, then given the chance to go home free. He stayed.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

As a child, Zayd ibn al-Haritha (may Allah be pleased with him) travelled with his mother to visit her family. They stopped for the night, and in the dark a band of raiders took everything, including the boy. He was sold into a slave market in Makkah, and his father would spend years not knowing whether his son was alive or dead.

Through a chain of hands, Zayd ended up in one household: the home of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. This was years before any revelation came. He was raised there not as a servant but as a son.

Then his real father found him. He came to Makkah ready to pay any price for his son's freedom. The Prophet ﷺ offered something better. Let Zayd choose, he said. If he wants to go with you, take him, and pay nothing.

Zayd answered without pause. "I will not choose anyone over you," he said. He was not choosing slavery over freedom. He said he had seen something in this man that he could never turn away from. So the Prophet ﷺ stood at the Kaaba and declared Zayd free, and called him his son. From that day a kidnapped boy of no rank was counted as a son of the Prophet ﷺ, and his father, who had only wanted his son's standing restored, was content.

That loyalty was tested in the hardest moments. When the Prophet ﷺ was beaten and turned away at Taif, only one person walked beside him, stepping in front of the blows. That was Zayd. He later became a trusted commander, sent at the head of every expedition he was given, and he is the only companion named by name in the whole Quran. He was killed leading the army at the battle of Mutah, and the Prophet ﷺ wept as he announced it.

There is a lesson in him that is easy to miss: loyalty shows itself most clearly when leaving would be easy. Zayd could have walked away free, back to his own father and home. He stayed for what he had seen in one man's character.

May Allah be pleased with him, and let us recognise good character the way he did.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: A boy travelled with his mother to visit her family. They stopped for the night and pitched their tent along the road. While they slept, a band of raiders fell upon them, took their food and money, and carried the boy away from his mother. His name was Zayd ibn al-Haritha (may Allah be pleased with him), and he would remember that night for the rest of his life.

They sold him quickly into a slave market in Makkah, before anyone could trace where he had come from. Far away, his father searched and could not find him. He wrote grieving poetry, wept outside his door, and asked only to know whether his son was alive or how he had died.

Through a chain of hands, Zayd was bought and then gifted into one household: the home of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, years before any revelation came. He was raised there as a son, no different from the Prophet's own children, and was shown such love that he would later say he was treated better than the family's own.

Then his father found him. He came to Makkah ready to pay any price for his son's freedom. But the Prophet ﷺ let Zayd choose, and offered to release him for nothing. Zayd answered at once: "I will not choose anyone over you." He was not choosing slavery over freedom. He had seen something in this man he could never turn away from.
So the Prophet ﷺ took him to the steps of the Kaaba and announced before the people that Zayd was free, and that he was his son. A kidnapped boy of no rank now belonged, in name and in heart.

That loyalty was tested. When the Prophet ﷺ was beaten and turned away at Taif on the worst day of his life, only Zayd walked beside him, stepping in front of the blows. He became a commander of the army, a husband and a father, and the only companion named by name in the whole Quran.

He was killed leading the Muslims at the battle of Mutah. The news reached the Prophet ﷺ before any messenger could carry it, and he climbed the pulpit and wept as he told his people that his son Zayd was gone.
storybook.pullquote: I will not choose anyone over you.
storybook.reflection: Zayd was handed a clear path back to freedom and family, and he refused it for something he could only name as the character he had seen in one man. Most of us are never asked to choose that dramatically, but we choose every day between what looks better and what is better. To recognise character, and to be loyal when loyalty costs something, is to walk a little of the road Zayd walked.
storybook.source: His story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
